_meta.interaction: QA workbench (kind: rubric, ĆWICZENIOWE — rubryka R4-qa-workbench, bez progu)
_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE dane SYNTETYCZNE (NotkaApp, SklepDemo, przyklad.test, PESEL placeholder 00000000000). ZERO realnych danych klienta/PII.
_meta.note: Egzemplarz typu rubric ĆWICZENIOWY: pominięto passThreshold i recordsPractical (feedback bez bramki). M8 obowiązkowy S2/S3, opcjonalny S1 — bez wariantu skróconego, ten sam ciąg ekranów dla wszystkich ścieżek. Treść interakcji oparta na SC-QA-01 (reset hasła NotkaApp).

intro: GenAI speeds up QA work: it drafts test cases, risk checklists, and documentation reviews. But an LLM generates the most probable text, not verified truth — which is why the tester remains the decision-maker, and the model an assistant.

screens.title: Why GenAI in QA — and where its role ends

blocks.text: An LLM speeds up creating and analyzing QA artifacts: test cases, risk checklists, documentation reviews. But it generates the most probable text, not verified truth — it is you who decides what ends up in the documentation.

items: E1 — you generate test cases from a requirement and point out coverage gaps.
E2 — you detect duplicates and sham tests (those with no real verification value).
E3 — you build a risk checklist (risk-based testing).
E4 — you judge whether an output is fit for QA documentation after a substantive review.
E5 — you identify where the LLM is an assistant and where a human makes the decision.

blocks.title: How this differs from M7
blocks.text: M7 taught how to write prompts. M8 focuses on applying an LLM to real QA tasks and assessing its output. The guiding principle: the LLM as assistant, the tester as decision-maker.

screens.title: Generating test cases from a requirement

blocks.text: From a synthetic requirement (e.g. "Password field: min. 8 characters, min. 1 digit") you ask the LLM for test cases: valid, boundary, negative. A good prompt provides the role, the requirement, and the output format (table: ID | steps | data | expected result).

items: Valid TC — the password meets all the rules, registration succeeds.
Boundary TC — a password of exactly the minimum length (8 characters).
Negative TC — a password with no digit, an error message expected.

blocks.title: Takeaway
blocks.text: The LLM gives a quick skeleton tied to acceptance criteria and to a requirement→test traceability link. It is a starting point, not a finished set.

screens.title: Coverage gaps — what the LLM did not think of

blocks.text: Read the output for coverage gaps: missing boundary cases, missing error paths, omitted states, no test data for extreme values. Technique: compare the generated cases against a list of risk categories and look for empty cells.

blocks.title: Synthetic example
blocks.text: For login the LLM covered the "happy path" but omitted the lockout after 5 failed attempts. What is usually missing are boundaries, error paths, and extreme data — that is where to look for gaps.

blocks.original: test with no real verification value
blocks.text: a case whose assertion checks nothing — no expected result, "the test always passes".
two cases checking the same conditions with a different description; they inflate the set without adding coverage.

blocks.title: Takeaway
blocks.text: More cases ≠ better coverage. A duplicate raises maintenance cost, and a sham test gives a false sense of safety.
The LLM drafts a risk checklist (risk-based testing) by category: security, data, performance, accessibility, compliance. Each item: risk + impact + area to test.

blocks.title: Synthetic checklist — the NotkaApp "password reset" feature
rows: Reset link works after expiry or multiple times
User e-mail exposed in an error message
Response content for an unknown account
Form not operable with a screen reader
blocks.textAlt: A risk checklist table for the NotkaApp password reset: category Security — risk of the link working after expiry or multiple times, area is 30-min validity and single-use; category Data — risk of the e-mail being exposed in an error, area is the response content for an unknown account; category Accessibility — risk of no screen-reader support, area is WCAG compliance, focus and labels.

blocks.text: The LLM proposes categories, the tester prioritizes and discards the off-target ones.

screens.title: Is this output fit for QA documentation?

blocks.text: Before pasting an output into documentation, run a substantive review (review checklist): consistency with the requirement, completeness, no fabricated facts (hallucinations), verifiability, no sensitive data.

items: Accept — the output is consistent with the requirement, complete, verifiable, with no sensitive data.
Fix — minor gaps or a single fabricated fact to correct against the requirement.
Reject — facts contradicting the requirement or sensitive data; do not use without rework.

blocks.text: A link to M11 (output verification) and M10 (data and privacy).

screens.title: Assistant or decision-maker — where to draw the line

blocks.text: The LLM can support tasks as an assistant: a draft of cases, a draft checklist, brainstorming. The human makes the decision (human-in-the-loop): accepting cases, assessing critical risk, sign-off for release.

blocks.title: Assistant vs decision-maker — 3 synthetic situations
rows: Draft of test cases from a requirement
blocks.textAlt: A table of three QA situations split into the role of assistant (LLM) and decision-maker (human): drafting test cases — the LLM generates a proposal, the human approves the scope; critical risk assessment — the LLM proposes categories, the human decides the priority; sign-off for release — the LLM does not decide, the human makes the decision.

blocks.title: The line
blocks.text: The higher the stakes of a decision (release, critical risk, closing a defect), the more firmly the role belongs to the human. The LLM speeds things up but does not sign off.

interaction.title: QA workbench
interaction.intro: NotkaApp requirement (fictional): a user resets their password via an e-mail link valid for 30 minutes; after 3 failed login attempts the account is locked for 15 minutes. The LLM generated these cases: TC1 — successful reset; TC2 — link expired after 30 min; TC3 — 3 wrong passwords → account locked. Assess this output against the rubric's five axes. This is a learning-mode exercise — you get feedback, with no pass threshold.

criteria.name: Test coverage
criteria.prompt: Which behaviors from the NotkaApp requirement does the LLM output actually COVER (TC1–TC3)?
criteria.hint: The output covers the reset happy path, link expiry after 30 min, and account lockout after 3 failed attempts.

options.text: Successful reset (TC1), link expiry after 30 min (TC2), and account lockout after 3 failed attempts (TC3).
Reuse of the same reset link and concurrent user sessions.
Cosmetics only: the color of the "Reset password" button and the logo on the login page.

criteria.name: Coverage gaps
criteria.prompt: Which cases are MISSING from the LLM output relative to the NotkaApp requirement? Mark exactly all the gaps.
criteria.hint: Three gaps were omitted: empty e-mail, a single-use link used twice, and concurrent sessions. Link expiry (TC2) and lockout after 3 attempts (TC3) are already covered.

options.text: Reset with an empty e-mail field (input validation).
Reuse of the same reset link (single-use link used twice).
Concurrent password-reset sessions for the same account.
Link expiry after 30 minutes — that already covers TC2.

criteria.name: Sham tests / duplicates
criteria.prompt: TC4 was added to the set: "Open the reset form and check that the form opens; expected result: none". How should it be classified?
criteria.hint: With no defined expected result, the assertion verifies nothing — this is a sham test, not a valuable boundary case.

options.text: A sham test — no expected result, the assertion actually checks nothing.
A valid boundary case for the link's 30-minute validity.
A valuable negative case for the account-lockout scenario.

criteria.name: Risks / wrong assumptions
criteria.prompt: In a note attached to the output the LLM wrote: "the account lockout lasts 24 hours, and the reset link is valid indefinitely". How should this be assessed against the requirement?
criteria.hint: The requirement says: 15-minute lockout, 30-minute link validity. The values 24 h and "indefinitely" are fabricated facts (hallucinations) contradicting the requirement — wrong assumptions to reject or fix.

options.text: Wrong assumptions contradicting the requirement (fabricated 24 h and "indefinitely") — fix or reject before use.
Acceptable — the LLM usually gives values closer to reality than the requirement does.
Accept with a note that the values may differ depending on the app version.

criteria.name: Decision boundary (human verification)
criteria.prompt: How should the roles between the LLM and the tester be split for this set, in line with the "assistant, not decision-maker" principle?
criteria.hint: The LLM drafts cases and a risk checklist; approving the scope, assessing critical risk, and the release decision belong to the human (human-in-the-loop).

options.text: The LLM generates a draft of cases and a risk checklist, and the tester approves the scope and decides on release sign-off.
The LLM signs off the release to production on its own if the generated tests pass.
The LLM ultimately assesses critical risk and closes defects without the tester's involvement.

points: E1 — from a requirement you generate a draft of test cases and read the output for coverage gaps (boundaries, error paths, extreme data).
E2 — you recognize duplicates (the same conditions with a different description) and sham tests (an assertion with no expected result); more cases ≠ better coverage.
E3 — you draft a risk checklist (security, data, performance, accessibility, compliance), and prioritize it yourself.
E4 — before documentation you apply a substantive review: accept / fix / reject (consistency with the requirement, no hallucinations, no sensitive data).
E5 — the LLM is an assistant; acceptance, critical risk, and release belong to the human (human-in-the-loop).
checklist: Does the output cover the happy path, the boundaries, and the error paths from the requirement?
Did I point out the coverage gaps as well as the duplicates and sham tests?
Did I check the output for facts contradicting the requirement (hallucinations)?
Have the critical-risk and release decisions stayed with the human?
summary.checklistTitle: Checklist — assessing LLM output in QA
summary.nextStep: Next step: M9 — integrations and agents (for S3 and optionally S2); for S1, a return to the mandatory path.
